prompt: Some people say it is always wrong to say a thing that is not true. But think about a person who says a thing that is not true to keep someone safe from being hurt.
If a person should always do the thing that brings the most good to the most people, then in some cases that person must do a thing that hurts one to help many.
How can we know that what we see and what we feel really tells us about the world? Maybe our minds make us see things that are not really there.

Think of two people walking by a small river. One person sees a child who is going to die in the water, and the other person does not. Now think about people who can give money to keep a child far away from dying.
Take a ship. Over many years, every part of it is changed for a new part. Is it still the same ship at the end?
If everything that happens is set by what came before, then can a person really pick what to do? Or are all picks just what was going to happen?
Some say it is the rule you follow that makes a thing right or wrong, not what comes out of it. Others say it is what comes out of it that matters, not the rule.
What does it mean to know a thing? Is it enough to think it is true and to be right? Or do you need more than that?
If a person's mind could be put into a different body, would the person still be the same person? Or would it be a new person?
Is it right to make one person feel a small bit of pain so that many people can feel a big bit of joy? What if the joy is very big and the pain is very small?
